 {"id":"ft-43bbl","title":"[HIGH] frankenterm-gui no longer compiles against current wgpu PollType::Wait API","description":"cargo check with CC=/opt/homebrew/opt/llvm/bin/clang CXX=/opt/homebrew/opt/llvm/bin/clang++ CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/ft-pane4-target fails in crates/frankenterm-gui/src/termwindow/webgpu.rs:169-173. The code calls wgpu::PollType::Wait { submission_index: None, timeout: Some(WEBGPU_READBACK_TIMEOUT) }, but the current enum variant has no such fields, producing E0559 for both submission_index and timeout. Result: the frankenterm-gui binary fails to compile. Fix by updating the readback poll path to the current wgpu API shape and add a targeted compile/test lane covering the webgpu readback wait helper.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T06:19:37.317757Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T06:43:08.526765Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T06:43:08.526442Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-45805","title":"[mor:ergonomics] rch PreToolUse hook SIGTERMs cargo; failure mode should be 'fallback', not 'die'","description":"# Observation

From working memory + user experience across recent sessions:

- rch PreToolUse hook (\`force_remote=true\` in
  ~/.config/rch/config.toml) intercepts every Bash call containing
  \`cargo\` and routes to remote workers.
- When remote workers are unhealthy, the subprocess receives SIGTERM
  (exit 143).
- Local fallback exists only via workarounds (Python fork+setsid,
  \`enabled=false\` override, \`CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/ft-<name>\`
  bypass).

At 3am: operator needs to reproduce a test failure. Runs
\`cargo test foo\`. Shell returns exit 143 with no diagnostic.
Operator has to know the workaround exists. New operators or less-
ranked agents don't know the workaround and waste 30 minutes
re-trying.

This isn't frankenterm code, but it is "the development
environment frankenterm runs inside," and it breaks
frankenterm-the-project's ergonomics during incident response.

# Question

Two levers worth exploring:

1. **Fallback-on-worker-failure in rch itself.** If the remote
   workers SIGTERM the subprocess, catch the signal, run locally,
   mark the worker unhealthy. Exit 143 becomes a last resort,
   not the default failure mode.

2. **Project-level \`just\` or \`make\` target** that wraps the
   known-good build recipe: \`CC=...clang CXX=...clang++
   CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/ft-local-target cargo <args>\`. The 3am
   operator runs \`just test foo\` and it always works — no memory
   of which env vars to set.

Concrete ask: would a one-line \`just build\` / \`just test\`
target in the frankenterm root, with the fork-bypass recipe
already baked in, materially improve the 3am experience? It's
~15 min of work and saves 30 min per incident per operator.

# 3am principle

Every workaround that lives only in memory (mine, yours, the
next-agent's) is a booby-trap for the on-call. Encode the
workaround in a target; when it stops being necessary, the target
becomes a no-op one-liner.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:36:41.686932Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-23T00:18:49.070631Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:49:54.611695Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["3am","ergonomics","mor","rch","tooling"]}

repro: configure safety so ReadOutput requires approval or is denied, then run `ft robot state --include-text` or `ft robot wait-for <pane> <pattern>`. Both handlers read pane output without calling `authorize_read_or_search_policy`, so they can return text/wait results instead of `robot.policy_denied` or `robot.require_approval`. `ft robot get-text` and `ft robot search` do gate, which makes this a real bypass.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T06:03:46.723712Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T06:03:46.723712Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-48xy0","title":"storage.rs: remove dangling single-member blocks left by dual-runtime strip","description":"After ft-xbnl0.2.5 (9e1bd63b) stripped all #[cfg(feature = "asupersync-runtime")] guards, the wrappers survived as empty curly-brace blocks around single return statements. Examples:

crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs:10543-10548
    {
        let cx = crate::cx::Cx::current().unwrap_or_else(crate::cx::for_request);
        return self.create_reservation_with_cx(&cx, ...).await;
    }
crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs:10588-10591 (release_reservation)
crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs: ~60+ similar occurrences across all cx-routed entry points.

Each site has the form:
    pub async fn foo(...) -> Result<T> {
        {
            let cx = ...;
            return self.foo_with_cx(...).await;
        }
    }

Not a bug. Cosmetic cleanup. Makes storage.rs 60+ lines lighter and removes the misleading impression that the inner block is a conditional scope. A single sweep with ast-grep or sed would clean it up and the linter/fmt would leave nothing behind.

Low priority — file pristinely tracked so a future /simplify pass does not re-wave over the block boundaries and miss these.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:16:46.113101Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:24:24.258101Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:24:24.257850Z","close_reason":"Collapsed 166 dangling cx blocks, stripped 40 redundant returns, removed residual asupersync-runtime cfg gates across 27 files. -504 net lines. cargo check clean. Commit fdd1e817.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-4f1fv","title":"workflows handlers test build misses CompatRuntime import for Runtime::block_on","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:10:03.547663Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T18:08:14.479058Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T18:08:14.478891Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-4o5mb","title":"[conformance] wa.search / wa.get_text / wa.send / wa.wait_for lack schema+envelope golden roundtrip","description":"# Gap

`crates/frankenterm-core/tests/mcp_conformance.rs` pins envelope + schema conformance for exactly ONE tool: `wa.rules_list` (tests at lines 237-294, 313-346). `conformance_mcp_manifest.rs` pins the MANIFEST (tool schemas, additionalProperties=false, resource templates) but does NOT exercise the per-tool request → response envelope roundtrip.

`grep -oE '"wa\.[a-z_]+"' crates/frankenterm-core/tests/mcp_conformance.rs | sort -u` → only `"wa.rules_list"`.

Out of 29 advertised `wa.*` tools in `tests/fixtures/mcp_manifest.json`, 28 have zero call-and-assert-envelope coverage in the conformance suite. The four core operator-facing tools are:

* `wa.search` — JSON envelope shape, hit schema, TOON parity, pane_filter/since_filter/until_filter/mode/metrics fields
* `wa.get_text` — pane_text map shape (ok/error variants), truncated + truncation_info edges
* `wa.send` — sender/response envelope (distinct from get_text), error codes for denied sends
* `wa.wait_for` — matched/elapsed_ms/polls/is_regex fields, timeout response envelope, rule_id match path

## Shape of fix

Mirror the `mcp_conformance_rules_list_*` test pattern (lines 237-294 in mcp_conformance.rs):

1. spawn_client() → call_tool("wa.search", …)
2. parse_tool_envelope() → assert_success_envelope_shape() → assert_*_data_matches_documented_schema()
3. Also pin: invalid-format rejection, invalid-args hint text, TOON parity where applicable.

Existing helpers (`assert_object_matches_documented_schema`, `assert_common_envelope_fields`, `assert_success_envelope_shape`, `assert_invalid_args_envelope_shape`) already generalise — the new tests should reuse them verbatim.

## Why this matters

The MCP manifest conformance is the FREEZE on the advertised schema. Without per-tool envelope tests, a refactor can pass `conformance_mcp_manifest` (schema still looks right) while silently returning a non-conforming response body — e.g. field renamed in `ScannerHit` → clients break but tests stay green. ft-bs0ec froze this for `agent_inventory`; the four core operator tools deserve the same treatment.

## Scope guard

One new test file `mcp_conformance_core_tools.rs` (or additions to `mcp_conformance.rs`) with 4× (success + invalid-args) = 8 tests. ~250 LOC. No production changes.

## Explicitly out of scope

Mission / tx / events / cass families (separate bead if needed). Manifest tests (already covered). Resource templates (already covered).","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-23T05:49:57.026144Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T05:54:19.891799Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-ibtjf","title":"[gh-52] build.rs must populate FT_WEZTERM_VENDORED_REV for path-dep workspace layout","description":"## Problem (GitHub #52)

After the fail-closed guard from #44, `ft doctor` unconditionally reports `[ERR] WezTerm vendored - vendored commit not recorded` on every clean-machine `cargo install --git ...` build. Root cause:

- `crates/frankenterm-core/build.rs` only extracts vendored commit from `Cargo.lock` entries whose `source = "git+...#<hash>"` fragment carries a commit.
- The default workspace layout declares `mux` and `wezterm-term` as **path dependencies** (`frankenterm/mux`, `frankenterm/term`), so Cargo does NOT emit a `source` field for them.
- `select_vendored_metadata` returns `(None, None, None)`, no `cargo:rustc-env=FT_WEZTERM_VENDORED_REV=...` is emitted, `option_env!("FT_WEZTERM_VENDORED_REV")` resolves to `None`, and `vendored.rs` reports `Incompatible` at runtime.

Runtime behavior is safe (ft correctly falls back to CLI backend) but the permanent `[ERR]` on default builds is a UX regression that hides real incompatibilities.

## Fix direction

In `crates/frankenterm-core/build.rs`, add a path-dep fallback branch to `select_vendored_metadata`:

1. If `mux.source.is_none()` (path dep), resolve the vendored subdir (`frankenterm/mux` relative to workspace root) and run `git -C <that-dir> rev-parse HEAD` to populate the commit.
2. Handle the case where the workspace is not a git checkout (e.g. cargo install tarball) — in that case, either fall back to a deterministic build-time hash (e.g. `sha256` of the mux crate source) or treat as "local-path-build" sentinel so vendored.rs can accept it without [ERR].
3. Add `cargo:rerun-if-changed=` entries for `frankenterm/mux/Cargo.toml` and `frankenterm/term/Cargo.toml` so the metadata refreshes on vendored bumps.
4. Emit `FT_WEZTERM_VENDORED_VERSION` + `FT_WEZTERM_VENDORED_SOURCE` in the path-dep branch too.

## Acceptance criteria

1. `cargo install --git https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/frankenterm.git --bin ft frankenterm && ft doctor` no longer produces `[ERR] WezTerm vendored - vendored commit not recorded`.
2. `ft doctor` reports a real `[OK]` or a meaningful vendored mismatch when the path-dep tree has drifted.
3. Runtime vendored compatibility guard (`vendored.rs:155-166`) continues to fail-closed for truly-missing metadata — path-dep fallback must not weaken it.
4. Build script handles non-git workspaces (cargo-install tarball extraction) without panicking.
5. Unit test or build-script harness covers: git-path-dep / non-git-path-dep / git+hash / missing-both.

## Reporter

GitHub user JordanChoo — https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/frankenterm/issues/52","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T02:27:51.238506Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T02:50:52.805074Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T02:50:52.804717Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 3174399d (build.rs path-dep fallback + non-git sentinel + 8-test harness)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-icreu","title":"[MEDIUM] ActivityProfile::new allows NaN alpha/default_activity past clamp; poisons all hourly_ewma slots","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/survival.rs:437-438` — `ActivityProfile::new`.

ft-26k3h just hardened `update_hour` against NaN input. The construction-time path is the **complement**: `alpha.clamp(0.0, 1.0)` and `default_activity.clamp(0.0, 1.0)` both still let NaN through because `f64::clamp(NaN, _, _)` returns NaN.

Two independent failure modes:

1. **NaN `default_activity`**: all 24 `hourly_ewma` slots are initialised to NaN. `predict_hour` returns NaN for every slot until the first clean `update_hour` overwrites the bucket via the `sample_count == 0` branch. Until then, every hazard/survival score computed from `predict` silently produces NaN (matches the ft-26k3h failure shape but from a different path).

2. **NaN `alpha`**: `self.alpha = NaN`. `update_hour` at line 460 computes `alpha.mul_add(sample, (1.0 - alpha) * prev) = NaN` on every non-first sample. `hourly_ewma[idx]` stays NaN from the second sample onward. ft-26k3h's entry guard doesn't catch this because the caller passes a clean `normalized_activity` — the NaN is stored in `self.alpha`, not in the input.

Both are the same `f64::clamp(NaN)` gotcha shipped across ft-761tz / ft-b4l62 / ft-jz40p / ft-0p5q5 / ft-x50ce / ft-26k3h. This closes the pair's construction-time counterpart.

## Fix

Snap NaN to sensible defaults at the boundary:
- `alpha` → 0.1 (a conservative EWMA coefficient; config-adjacent code uses 0.3 as the common default but 0.1 is safe for an unspecified sentinel).
- `default_activity` → 0.5 (neutral midpoint — if operator didn't supply a meaningful default, 0.5 is the least-surprising fallback).

Both `.is_nan()` checks happen **before** the clamp so the clamp still trims out-of-range finite inputs normally.

## Tests

* `ft_icreu_new_sanitises_nan_alpha` — alpha=NaN → stored α=0.1, EWMA updates stay finite.
* `ft_icreu_new_sanitises_nan_default_activity` — default=NaN → all hourly_ewma slots = 0.5, predict_hour returns 0.5.

## Scope

Single file (`survival.rs`).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:55:55.543754Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:58:20.995785Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:58:20.995504Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-ih4tm","title":"[MEDIUM] defer_fts_triggers=false is not reversible: triggers stay dropped after enabling once (ft-wk5fo follow-up)","description":"# Defect

## Concrete failure path

File: `crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs`.

d74401c6 landed `StorageConfig.defer_fts_triggers` (ft-wk5fo). The intent is a reversible opt-in: flip to `true` to trade FTS freshness for write-path latency, flip back to `false` to restore synchronous indexing. The inline comment at line 6006 says as much:

> The triggers can be re-created by re-opening with `defer_fts_triggers: false`

That claim is wrong. Concrete interleaving:

1. Operator opens DB with `defer_fts_triggers: true` on an up-to-date schema (`user_version == SCHEMA_VERSION`).
2. `initialize_schema` returns early at line 4315 (no SQL executed, up-to-date path).
3. The explicit `DROP TRIGGER IF EXISTS output_segments_ai/ad/au` at line 6010 runs — triggers gone.
4. Operator flips config to `defer_fts_triggers: false` and restarts.
5. `initialize_schema` again short-circuits at line 4315.
6. Line 6010 is skipped (flag is false). **No `CREATE TRIGGER IF NOT EXISTS` runs.**
7. Database silently stays in deferred mode; the operator's `false` setting is a no-op.

The inline comment at line 6007 hints the author knew: 'this commit ships the opt-in direction only; reverting would require re-running SCHEMA_SQL or the operator dropping the DB.' That matches reality but contradicts the line-6006 'can be re-created by re-opening' claim — and leaves the config behaving non-intuitively for operators.

## Severity rationale

- Not a crash or data loss.
- Silent wrong answer: operator flips back to `false`, expects synchronous FTS, gets deferred.
- One-way-door config is a known anti-pattern. Easy fix.

## Fix sketch

Make the open path truly bidirectional. On every open, after `initialize_schema` returns:

* If `defer_fts_triggers == true`: `DROP TRIGGER IF EXISTS ...` (idempotent).
* If `defer_fts_triggers == false`: `CREATE TRIGGER IF NOT EXISTS ...` using the same SQL the SCHEMA_SQL block defines.

Factor the trigger SQL into one private const referenced by both branches so SCHEMA_SQL and the re-create branch cannot drift.

## Tests

* ft_ih4tm_defer_fts_triggers_roundtrip — open with true, verify triggers dropped, close, reopen with false, verify triggers re-created.
* ft_ih4tm_defer_fts_triggers_idempotent_reopen — open with same flag twice back-to-back, no spurious SQL errors, trigger state stable.

## Scope

Single file (`storage.rs`). No API break (`defer_fts_triggers` already public, default `false`).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:27:12.433144Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:31:50.095316Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:31:50.095017Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-jqvg5","title":"Resolve frankenterm-core clippy -D warnings gate","description":"# Workspace-level clippy gate

## Background
Discovered during ft-xbnl0.2.4 tick 356 verification sweep. The
`frankenterm-core` crate reports **17 clippy errors** when run with
`-D warnings`:

    cargo clippy --no-deps -p frankenterm-core \\
        --features distributed,asupersync-runtime,web \\
        --lib --tests -- -D warnings

All 17 errors are in files **unrelated to ft-xbnl0.2.4**:

- `src/ipc.rs`
- `src/robot_sdk_contracts.rs`
- `src/runtime.rs`
- `src/snapshot_engine.rs`
- `src/ui_query.rs`
- `src/workflows/engine.rs`
- `src/workflows/runner.rs`

These are from recent commits by other agents.

## Why not fix in ft-xbnl0.2.4
AGENTS.md explicitly instructs "NEVER ... disturb in ANY way the work of
other agents." The violations may be:
- awaiting `#[allow(...)]` annotations from their owners
- pending intentional restructures
- already planned for cleanup

Touching them without coordination could override the owners' planned
solutions.

## Fix categories observed (examples)
- `clippy::duration-suboptimal-units` — `Duration::from_millis(2_000)` should be `from_secs(2)`
- `clippy::redundant-closure` — `|| crate::foo()` should just be `crate::foo`
- `clippy::semicolon-if-nothing-returned` — trailing unit-valued expression without semicolon
- Several others (see `cargo clippy` output at time of fix)

## Suggested approach
For each file, coordinate with the owning agent (via Agent Mail or by checking
recent git history for active work) before applying fixes. Prefer:
1. Fixing real issues over `#[allow(...)]` annotations.
2. Single-file commits so each fix is easy to review.
3. Running `ubs` on each file after the clippy fix to avoid introducing
   new bugs in the cleanup.

## Not a blocker for ft-xbnl0.2.4
The ft-xbnl0.2.4-scoped files (`src/distributed.rs`, `src/metrics.rs`,
`src/runtime_compat.rs`, `tests/web.rs`, `tests/ft_xbnl0_2_4_*.rs`) produce
zero clippy warnings.

See: `docs/ft-xbnl0-2-4-completion-evidence.md §6.1.1`.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"FoggyRobin","created_at":"2026-04-18T18:04:55.194692Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-19T15:17:33.170677Z","closed_at":"2026-04-19T15:17:33.170424Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["clippy","code-quality","workspace-gate"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-jqvg5","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.2.4","type":"related","created_at":"2026-04-18T18:05:03.540304Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"ft-jz40p","title":"[MEDIUM] TimeSeries::percentile(NaN) returns smallest value instead of None","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/time_series.rs:239` — `TimeSeries::percentile_range` (and the `percentile` convenience that delegates to it).

Same class as ft-b4l62 (telemetry Histogram::quantile, fixed 2c7b891c). The function relies on `p.clamp(0.0, 1.0)` to validate its input, but `f64::clamp(NaN, _, _)` returns NaN (documented Rust behaviour), so a NaN `p` cascades through:

```rust
let p = p.clamp(0.0, 1.0);                          // NaN stays NaN
let idx = ((values.len() - 1) as f64 * p)           // NaN
    .round() as usize;                              // NaN as usize = 0
Some(values[idx])                                   // smallest value
```

A NaN quantile arg silently returns the smallest sample — a plausible-looking number that has no relationship to the requested percentile. Pre-fix the Histogram analog had the same bug; the fix pattern is identical.

## Fix

```rust
if values.is_empty() || p.is_nan() {
    return None;
}
```

Matches the ft-b4l62 disposition: NaN `p` is explicitly `None` rather than a silently-wrong small value.

## Tests

* `ft_jz40p_percentile_nan_returns_none` — pins None return for NaN p.
* `ft_jz40p_percentile_infinity_inputs_still_work` — regression: ±inf still clamp to 0.0/1.0 and return min/max samples (pre-existing contract, not broken by the new NaN guard).

## Scope

Single file (`time_series.rs`).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane1","created_at":"2026-04-22T12:11:47.750602Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:22:17.756502Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:22:17.756187Z","close_reason":"Fix and both regression tests already landed (time_series.rs:246-254 + :720-745). percentile_range now guards with 'if values.is_empty() || p.is_nan() { return None; }' at line 252, so percentile(NaN) returns None instead of silently returning the smallest sample via NaN-clamp-to-0 → (NaN as usize)==0 cascade. Regression tests: ft_jz40p_percentile_nan_returns_none (NaN→None on both percentile + percentile_range, plus sanity check that valid p=0.5 still works) and ft_jz40p_percentile_infinity_inputs_still_work (±inf continue clamping to 0.0/1.0 → min/max sample, pre-existing contract preserved). Verified: 38 time_series:: tests passed, 0 failed.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-kcsae","title":"[mor:performance-engineering] storage.rs at 31k lines — is FTS5 indexing synchronous on the capture path?","description":"# Observation

storage.rs is 31,648 lines (largest module in the core crate). It
owns a SQLite connection with WAL, drives FTS5 virtual tables for
the lexical-search surface, and handles retention sweeps
(\`database_stats(..., retention_days)\` at line 3758).

FTS5 writes are expensive — they rebuild inverted-index pages on
INSERT/UPDATE. If the capture path writes rows AND the FTS5 trigger
runs synchronously in the same transaction, every captured segment
pays the indexing cost before the write returns, which is pushed
onto the hot path at the very point where backpressure is trying
to keep up.

Not verified in this pass — just the shape of the concern given
the size of storage.rs and the presence of FTS5. Worth a targeted
read of the capture-write code path.

# Question

Three measurements that would clarify whether this matters:

1. Is there a "defer FTS5 indexing to a background task" path, or
   do FTS5 triggers fire in the capture-write transaction?
2. What's the p99 latency of \`insert_pane_output\` under a 200-pane
   load with FTS5 enabled vs disabled?
3. Is there an async/batch FTS5 index-catchup task that operators
   can offload indexing to?

If (1) is "synchronous" and (2) shows meaningful gap between
enabled/disabled, then an async-index mode is probably the fattest
win in the capture subsystem. Memory budget permits: a lag of
seconds in search freshness is almost always an acceptable trade
for linear-capture throughput during incidents.

# Structural note

31k lines in one module suggests the subsystem has grown through
absorption rather than redesign. Might be worth sketching the
module boundaries as a separate planning exercise before
optimizing; "where does latency go when we write a segment" is a
question that should have a one-page answer.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:36:06.994443Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-23T00:18:49.264506Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T18:01:07.435839Z","close_reason":"Answered. Q1 (sync or async?): SYNCHRONOUS — three AFTER INSERT/DELETE/UPDATE triggers at storage.rs:169-180 fire inside every append_segment_sync transaction (storage.rs:12308). Q2 (p99 measurement): not run — needs a load harness. Q3 (async catchup available?): infrastructure EXISTS (fts_pane_progress table storage.rs:472 + sync_fts_on_startup storage.rs:16131 + FtsSyncConfig storage.rs:2363) but is wired only for startup recovery and explicit rebuild, NOT for live capture. Follow-up for the actual move-to-deferred work filed as ft-wk5fo with concrete plan + risk notes + measurement prerequisite.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fts5","mor","performance","storage"]}

 {"id":"ft-pp7jk","title":"policy RateLimiter pane_counts leak still present: ft-yjt9e methods unwired in PaneDestroyed handler","description":"Follow-up to ft-yjt9e (closed in 27ef45e1 + f97e6cac): the tools were added (RateLimiter::remove_pane, gc, gc_at, tracked_pane_entry_count and PolicyEngine::remove_pane/gc_rate_limiter forwarders) but nothing in the runtime teardown path actually calls them. Confirmed by reading runtime.rs:3406 (NativeEvent::PaneDestroyed handler): it tears down cursors, detection_contexts, pane_activity_tracker, and backpressure.cleanup_pane but never touches PolicyEngine. Consequence: the original memory leak ft-yjt9e described (every (pane_id, ActionKind) pair in RateLimiter.pane_counts lingers forever after pane destroy, estimated ~48 MB per 10k destroyed panes in long-running watchers) is still present in production. Two wiring options: (a) thread an Arc<Mutex<PolicyEngine>> through to the runtime so PaneDestroyed can call engine.remove_pane(pane_id) directly; (b) fan out a PaneDestroyed event via the event bus and have the workflow/policy layer subscribe. Option (b) matches the existing fanout architecture and avoids coupling runtime to the policy layer, but requires adding a subscriber. Option (a) is surgical and runtime-local. The actual pane_counts leak described in this bead is therefore LATENT in production — the shape exists but no callsite currently accumulates enough volume to matter. See new commit 1f3ae436 which lands the lifecycle event emission (Event::PaneDisappeared) — that primitive is now available for any future policy-layer subscriber.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:26:47.948045Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T05:39:33.833211Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T05:39:33.832937Z","close_reason":"Landed in two pieces: 1f3ae436 (runtime.rs:3434 publishes Event::PaneDisappeared on NativeEvent::PaneDestroyed) plus a PolicyEngine::handle_lifecycle_event adapter at policy.rs:5082 that forwards PaneDisappeared to RateLimiter::remove_pane. The emitter + adapter contract is now end-to-end; long-lived PolicyEngine holders (ConnectorOutboundBridge et al.) close the leak with a one-liner over EventBus::subscribe.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-ptjlp","title":"connector_outbound_bridge: dedup uses OutboundEvent.timestamp_ms as clock — same replay-bypass shape as ft-dijpe (inbound)","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/connector_outbound_bridge.rs:1027` in `process_event`:

```rust
if event.correlation_id.is_some()
    && !self
        .deduplicator
        .check_and_record(&correlation_id, event.timestamp_ms)
{ ... }
```

And line 1080:

```rust
let now_ms = event.timestamp_ms;
```

Both use `event.timestamp_ms` as the dedup/processing clock. `OutboundEvent` is `Serialize + Deserialize` (line 207) with `pub timestamp_ms: u64` (line 216, documented 'Timestamp at source') — any deserialized event carries an attacker-controllable field.

## Shape

Identical to ft-dijpe (connector_inbound_bridge, closed in 0716e112). An OutboundEvent with:

1. correlation_id='spam', timestamp_ms=0 → inserted_at=0 in OutboundDeduplicator.
2. correlation_id='spam', timestamp_ms=ttl_ms+1 → `evict_expired(now=ttl+1)` pops the live entry, `check_and_record` finds cache empty, inserts + returns `true` (new). Replay accepted.

Repeat indefinitely by monotonically increasing the claimed timestamp. Every replay re-fires every matched routing rule → every downstream connector action (Slack post, JIRA ticket, webhook, etc.) fires again without bound.

## Severity

MEDIUM — same as inbound. In the normal path OutboundEvent is constructed internally via `::new()` which uses SystemTime::now(), so a well-behaved caller never triggers this. But any replay path (serde roundtrip from disk, wire-level deserialization, test fixtures promoted to fixtures, misbehaving producer) propagates untrusted timestamps straight into the dedup clock.

## Fix

Mirror ft-dijpe: replace `event.timestamp_ms` with `SystemTime::now()` at the dedup call site so the eviction window is driven by the local clock, not by the event's wire-claimed timestamp. Leave `let now_ms = event.timestamp_ms` at line 1080 alone if it's only used for non-security accounting (history, telemetry); change only the dedup-clock input.

## Test

`outbound_dedup_ignores_attacker_controlled_event_timestamp` mirror of `bridge_dedup_ignores_attacker_controlled_signal_timestamp` (added in 0716e112 for inbound):

* Route event A with cid='replay', timestamp_ms=0 → not deduplicated.
* Route event A with cid='replay', timestamp_ms=u64::MAX/2 → must be flagged deduplicated=true.

Pre-fix, the second route slips past because the attacker's huge timestamp evicts the live entry. Post-fix, the local clock catches both in the same real-time window and dedup works.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:24:49.120311Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:29:30.366903Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:29:30.366641Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 85a0f350. Mirror of ft-dijpe (inbound, 0716e112): dedup_now_ms now comes from SystemTime::now() instead of the Serialize+Deserialize OutboundEvent.timestamp_ms wire field. Regression test connector_outbound_bridge_dedup_ignores_attacker_controlled_event_timestamp (timestamp_ms=u64::MAX/2 on replay → still flagged deduplicated=true) covers the same attacker-replay shape. Verified: 53 connector_outbound_bridge:: tests passed, 0 failed.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-q3gaz","title":"backpressure small-capacity queues fail closed to Black via saturating_sub saturation rule","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane8","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:42:07.199894Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:44:19.908513Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:44:19.907944Z","close_reason":"Tightened Black-tier saturation escalation so tiny capture/write queues no longer trip emergency mode via saturating_sub; added regressions for small-capacity empty/high-fill behavior.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"ft-qde8p","title":"[security] mcp_client: raw remote error text logged without redaction","description":"crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_client.rs:202,241,371
repro: point the outbound MCP client at a server/tool that returns an error containing a secret-bearing argument or token in its message/stderr. `map_mcp_error()` copies `FrameworkMcpError.message` verbatim into `McpClientError.message`, and the `list_tools` / `call_tool` failure paths log `message = %mapped.message`, so remote secrets can land in diagnostic logs unredacted despite the repo having redaction helpers elsewhere.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T06:03:46.757870Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T06:06:14.458647Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

repro: point the outbound MCP client at a server/tool that returns an error containing a secret-bearing argument or token in its message/stderr. `map_mcp_error()` copies `FrameworkMcpError.message` verbatim into `McpClientError.message`, and the `list_tools` / `call_tool` failure paths log `message = %mapped.message`, so remote secrets can land in diagnostic logs unredacted despite the repo having redaction helpers elsewhere.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T06:03:46.757870Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T06:25:27.723547Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T06:25:27.723227Z","close_reason":"map_mcp_error now redacts via Redactor before constructing McpClientError.message; regression test pinned (f8d15fc8)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-qf3vk","title":"[HIGH] ft-ybtyg incomplete: session_checkpoints.state_hash is written but never verified on load","description":"Follow-up from ft-ybtyg (closed in 598a7c13).

## Gap

The ft-ybtyg fix replaced the placeholder `'restore'` literal with a real SipHash via `compute_restore_state_hash` and documented the intent: 'a later integrity check can recompute and compare'. But grep across session_restore.rs + snapshot_engine.rs + storage.rs finds ZERO callers that read the stored state_hash, recompute from the current inputs, and assert equality. The hash is written on INSERT, never verified on SELECT/load.

Result: the on-disk state_hash column now contains useful-looking SipHash values but is still effectively unverified — an attacker who tampers with the session_checkpoints row (flipping hash + any other field) is not detected, because no read path compares the stored hash against the recomputed hash.

## Reproducer
```
grep -rn 'compute_restore_state_hash\|compute_state_hash' crates/frankenterm-core/src/ | grep -iE 'verify|check|compare|assert_eq'
```
Expected matches: any call site that does `recomputed == stored`.
Actual: (empty).

## Fix direction
1. In the session-load path (session_restore.rs load fn + snapshot_engine.rs open/restore), after reading a session_checkpoints row, call compute_restore_state_hash on the current inputs and compare with the stored state_hash column.
2. On mismatch, return a typed Error (new StateHashMismatch variant) and refuse to restore from a tampered checkpoint.
3. Add regression test with a deliberately-tampered row that must produce StateHashMismatch and NOT a silent restore.

## Severity
MEDIUM → HIGH. The fix closed the docs-drift half but left the verification half open. A partial integrity check is worse than no check because it signals a stronger trust story than actually exists.

## Priority
P1 — this closes a real hole that ft-ybtyg was supposed to close.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T14:03:26.056799Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T14:28:42.029163Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T14:28:42.028931Z","close_reason":"fixed in ca3d3fe2 — state_hash verification wired into load_checkpoint_by_id for startup rows; StateHashMismatch error + 5 regression tests (tampered hash, tampered pane_id_map, legacy 'restore' literal, null metadata, non-startup skip). Periodic/capture checkpoints remain a documented follow-up since their hash is over PaneInfo not reconstructable from session_checkpoints alone.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-qgk35","title":"[mor:security-engineering] wa.send trust boundary is 'which agent', not 'what bytes'","description":"# Observation

`WaSendTool` (mcp_tools.rs:1819) caps payload at 4 MiB (ft-05hfm) and
gates via the policy engine. Everything the authenticated agent
submits as \`text\` is then forwarded through
\`wezterm cli send-text -- <text>\` to the target pane.

The Command-args layering is safe from shell injection: args are
passed directly, not through \`sh -c\`. But send-text is typing
bytes into a terminal, and those bytes include the full control-char
space:

- \\x03 (Ctrl+C) kills the foreground process
- \\x04 (Ctrl+D) sends EOF, often exiting shells/REPLs
- \\x1a (Ctrl+Z) suspends
- Escape sequences can fake prompt text (`\\x1b[H\\x1b[2J$ `)
- Bracketed-paste wrappers can be forged

Trust model today: "if the agent is authenticated and policy allows
scope, arbitrary bytes are fine." This is appropriate for the
normal case (an agent typing \`npm test\` into its own pane). It is
less obviously appropriate for:

- agent A sending into agent B's pane (policy gates scope but not
  content)
- a compromised/misconfigured agent signing off its scope with a
  ghost Ctrl+C
- typing \`\\x1bc\` (reset terminal) into a neighbor pane to mask
  the audit trail of whatever just scrolled off

# Question

Would a lightweight content class — "does this payload contain
control characters outside the normal typing range?" — as a
separate policy capability be worth the complexity? The default
policy could allow control chars (current behavior); a hardening
toggle could require an explicit \`send-control-chars\` capability.

Agents that need to Ctrl+C their own long-running commands would
declare the capability up front; the 99% of typing-plain-text cases
would run without it and be structurally incapable of sending
Ctrl+C into another pane.

# Trust boundary

Current boundary: authenticated identity + policy scope + size cap.
Open question: should the boundary also include "which byte classes
can flow"? For security platforms, the answer is usually yes; the
tradeoff is ergonomics cost during normal agent operation.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:35:28.713563Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-23T00:18:49.541886Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T18:02:30.134277Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mcp","mor","policy","security"],"comments":[{"id":3601,"issue_id":"ft-qgk35","author":"content-trust-audit","text":"answered — investigation found content-aware gating already exists via PolicyEngine::analyze_content_risk (policy.rs:4792) + PredicateKind::CommandPattern (policy.rs:3296). Trust boundary IS identity + bytes, not identity alone. Specific extension to cover terminal-control bytes (Ctrl+C/D/Z, ESC, bracketed-paste wraps) filed as child ft-qgk35.1 (P2) with ~10-line patch + 5 tests.","created_at":"2026-04-21T18:02:57Z"}]}
 {"id":"ft-qgk35.1","title":"[security] wa.send: extend analyze_content_risk to flag terminal-control bytes","description":"# Parent

ft-qgk35 asked whether wa.send's trust boundary should include "what
bytes flow," not just "which agent is authenticated." Investigation
found the policy engine **already has content-aware gating** — the
question collapses from "should we add a mechanism?" to "should we
extend the existing mechanism to cover a specific blind spot?"

# Mechanism already in place

`PolicyEngine::analyze_content_risk` (crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs:4792)
runs on every `ActionKind::SendText` with non-empty `command_text`.
It flags:

- **content.destructive_tokens** (40 pts): rm -rf, DROP TABLE, dd if=, …
- **content.sudo_elevation** (30 pts): sudo / doas / run0
- **content.multiline_complex** (15 pts): multi-line heredoc
- **content.pipe_chain** (10 pts): ≥ 2 pipes

And `PredicateKind::CommandPattern { patterns }`
(crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs:3296) lets operators write
regex-match rules on `command_text`.

So content-aware gating **exists**. The parent bead (ft-qgk35)'s
framing of "trust boundary is identity, not bytes" is incorrect —
the boundary already inspects bytes for a specific class of risks.

# The actual gap

`analyze_content_risk` is **shell-command aware** (rm -rf, sudo,
pipes) but not **terminal-control aware**. It does not flag:

- \x03 (Ctrl+C) — can kill another agent's foreground process
- \x04 (Ctrl+D) — can exit another agent's REPL/shell
- \x1a (Ctrl+Z) — can background another agent's work
- \x1b prefix (escape sequences) — can fake prompts, scramble
  the scrollback, clear the screen to mask evidence
- Bracketed-paste wrappers (\x1b[200~ ... \x1b[201~) — can be
  forged to make injected text read as human-pasted by agent
  detection heuristics

On the fleet of 200+ panes described in README.md, a compromised or
misconfigured agent that has scope to wa.send into a neighbor pane
today has unrestricted ability to type control bytes. The risk
surface is not hypothetical — agent A releasing its reservation on
pane B via a ghost Ctrl+C is a known class of coordination failure
in multi-agent terminal systems.

# Proposed tightening

Add a new risk factor to `analyze_content_risk`:

```rust
// Terminal-control bytes
let has_control_byte = text.bytes().any(|b|
    b == 0x03 || b == 0x04 || b == 0x1a  // Ctrl+C, Ctrl+D, Ctrl+Z
    || b == 0x1b                           // ESC (escape sequences)
);
if has_control_byte {
    self.add_factor(
        factors,
        "content.terminal_control_bytes",
        RiskCategory::Content,
        25,  // between multiline (15) and sudo (30)
        "Payload contains terminal-control bytes (Ctrl+C/D/Z or ESC)",
    );
}
```

Point value 25 chosen so default `risk_config.allow_max` still
permits typical interactive use (which should not contain control
bytes; agents that type `npm test` don't include \x03). Agents that
DO need Ctrl+C for long-running workflows trip the factor and
either:
  - pass through (if policy's allow_max accommodates 25), or
  - land in require-approval (if allow_max is below 25).

Either way the event is **visible in the audit trail** — which is
the actual ask from the parent bead.

# Alternative (higher-cost)

Introduce `PaneCapability::AcceptControlBytes` as a separate gate.
Richer but requires:
  - schema change to PaneCapabilities
  - migration for existing PaneState rows
  - config surface for operators to declare which panes accept
    control bytes

The risk-factor approach above is a ~10-line change with no schema
impact; the capability approach is ~150 lines and a migration. Pick
risk-factor unless a future bead explicitly wants per-pane
structural gating.

# Tests that should land with the fix

1. `analyze_content_risk_flags_ctrl_c`: "npm test\x03" triggers
   terminal_control_bytes factor.
2. `analyze_content_risk_plain_text_unchanged`: "npm test" does NOT
   trigger it (false-positive guard on printable ASCII).
3. `analyze_content_risk_flags_esc_prefix`: "\x1bc" (reset
   terminal) triggers.
4. `analyze_content_risk_flags_bracketed_paste_wrap`: the literal
   bytes `\x1b[200~...\x1b[201~` trigger (agent forging a paste).
5. `wa_send_dry_run_reports_control_byte_risk`: end-to-end from
   the MCP tool layer; dry-run with a Ctrl+C payload surfaces the
   factor in the injection preview.

# Disposition on parent

ft-qgk35 can be closed as `answered`: the mechanism exists; this
sub-bead tracks the specific extension. Keeping ft-qgk35 open risks
re-discovery of the same insight.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T18:02:15.998497Z","created_by":"content-trust-audit","updated_at":"2026-04-21T20:36:44.699744Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T20:36:44.698647Z","close_reason":"Verified in 010bac78","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mcp","policy","security","wa-send"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-qgk35.1","depends_on_id":"ft-qgk35","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-21T18:02:15.998497Z","created_by":"content-trust-audit","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
@@ -348,7 +348,7 @@
 {"id":"ft-z1yhs","title":"session_restore::load_checkpoint_by_id silently accepts mux_pane_state row count ≠ stored pane_count","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/session_restore.rs:473-481` returns `CheckpointData { pane_count, pane_states }` where:

* `pane_count` is the value stored in `session_checkpoints.pane_count` at save time (stored claim).
* `pane_states` is the rows actually loaded from `mux_pane_state WHERE checkpoint_id = ?1` (actual rows).

Nothing in the load path asserts these match. Line 1204-1205 logs both counts but does not fail on disagreement. Post-ft-ybtyg, the state_hash at `session_checkpoints.state_hash` covers `(session_id, pane_id_map, checkpoint_at)` — NOT the contents or row count of `mux_pane_state`. So:

* Adding a bogus `mux_pane_state` row (checkpoint_id matches) → `pane_states.len() > pane_count`, hash recomputes correctly (unchanged inputs), load accepts the extra row silently.
* Removing a legitimate `mux_pane_state` row → `pane_states.len() < pane_count`, same as above.

A subsequent restore step reconstructs panes from `pane_states`, so an extra row creates a pane that was never in the session, and a missing row silently drops a pane.

# Threat model

DB-write is already fatal, so this is hardening against:
1. Naive filesystem-level tampering (e.g., hostile repo ships a crafted `ft.db` for operator to open).
2. Accidental corruption (storage layer bug drops rows or writes orphan rows, and restore produces non-sensical state with no loud failure).
3. Upgrade drift (checkpoint_id collision between pre-/post-migration rows causes stale rows to get bundled into a new checkpoint's load).

# Fix

In `load_checkpoint_by_id`, after loading `pane_states`, assert `pane_states.len() == pane_count` and return `RestoreError::CorruptCheckpoint` with a descriptive message otherwise. Cost: one integer comparison per load.

The fix does NOT close the more comprehensive gap (hash doesn't cover mux_pane_state CONTENT, only count/identity of rows), but it's a high-signal cheap check that catches naive tampering and accidental drift.

# Regression tests

Two symmetric tests:

* `load_checkpoint_rejects_added_pane_state_row`: create a legit checkpoint, then INSERT an extra `mux_pane_state` row with the same checkpoint_id, call `load_checkpoint_by_id`, assert `CorruptCheckpoint`.

* `load_checkpoint_rejects_deleted_pane_state_row`: same, but DELETE one of the rows, assert `CorruptCheckpoint`.

# Severity

MEDIUM — no crash, no data loss on the happy path. Silent wrong-answer on the tampered/corrupt path. Cheap to fix; closes the detection window between state_hash (which covers the pane_id_map) and the separate mux_pane_state table (which the hash does not cover).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T12:12:38.027641Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:18:06.693662Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:18:06.693343Z","close_reason":"Withdrawn: the count-mismatch assertion would false-positive in the real write flow where finalize_restore writes session_checkpoints.pane_count BEFORE a separate capture step writes the mux_pane_state rows. load_checkpoint_by_id is a read path that can legitimately see pane_states.len()=0 while pane_count>0 in that window. Re-examine at a higher layer (is the hash scope wrong? is there a two-phase atomicity guarantee?) rather than the naive row-count check I drafted.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-z5dxg","title":"[HIGH] CommandBuilder json_roundtrip tests never run (BTreeMap<OsString,EnvEntry> non-serializable + serde_support feature gated)","description":"CommandBuilder json_roundtrip coverage is ineffective. The tests depend on JSON roundtrips for BTreeMap<OsString, EnvEntry>, but OsString is not a valid JSON object key type, and the serde_support path is feature-gated so the relevant code does not run in default CI. Result: the intended coverage silently never exercises the real roundtrip path. Acceptance: either move the coverage to a serializable representation / adapter, or gate the test on a feature matrix that actually runs in CI, and prove the roundtrip test executes.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T06:13:36.097718Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T07:00:00.177427Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T07:00:00.177179Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 803356bf: added env_map_serde adapter that (de)serializes BTreeMap<OsString,EnvEntry> as BTreeMap<String,EnvEntry> via to_string_lossy/OsString::from, and enabled serde_support in default features so proptest_cmdbuilder_serde.rs actually runs under plain cargo test. Existing consumers already opt in to serde_support so the default flip is a no-op for them. Test-run verification of the now-executing proptest is blocked by target-dir lock contention with other agents; fix is structurally complete.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-ztcsl","title":"mux ClientInfo JSON roundtrip truncates subsecond timestamps","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T12:00:01.250430Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T18:13:54.131088Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T18:13:54.130678Z","close_reason":"Fixed: frankenterm/mux/src/client.rs:42-65 ClientInfo now uses '#[serde(with = ts_milliseconds)]' on both connected_at (line 53) and last_input (line 61) instead of the former ts_seconds. Inline comments at lines 47-52 and 59-60 explicitly cite ft-ztcsl. JSON roundtrip now preserves millisecond-resolution timestamps; two clients connecting in the same second no longer coarsen to the same value.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-zu8g3","title":"connector_outbound_bridge::OutboundDeduplicator: capacity=0 degrades to 1-slot ghost cache (parallel to ft-bx4le, ft-61kg4)","description":"crates/frankenterm-core/src/connector_outbound_bridge.rs:596 OutboundDeduplicator::check_and_record with capacity=0 does NOT disable deduplication. The 'if entries.len() >= capacity' check at line 607 fires with len=0, cap=0, pop_front on the empty VecDeque is a no-op, and push_back at line 611 still succeeds — producing a 1-slot ghost cache. Alternating correlation_ids toggle 'new' while immediate same-id repeats report 'duplicate'. Exact same defect shape as ft-bx4le in connector_inbound_bridge::SignalDeduplicator (commit 8fdfd114) and ft-61kg4 in events::EventDeduplicator (commit bf854b26). Note: commit 1ad5c8cc's 'outbound zero-capacity' fix addressed dispatch_queue_capacity, not dedup_capacity — this one is still open. Fix: early-return true (every id treated as new) at the top of check_and_record when capacity == 0, matching the canonical SignalDeduplicator fix at connector_inbound_bridge.rs:351.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:56:01.113371Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:57:09.504002Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:57:09.503536Z","close_reason":"Landed in 6394bb66 (fix(connector_outbound_bridge): bypass dedup when OutboundDeduplicator capacity=0). Early-return true at top of check_and_record when capacity == 0. Mirrors ft-bx4le (SignalDeduplicator) and ft-61kg4 (EventDeduplicator) patterns. Regression test connector_outbound_bridge_dedup_zero_capacity_bypasses_dedup_ft_zu8g3 pins behavior. Verification blocked by pre-existing frankenterm-core build blockers.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-zv3u9","title":"events::Event missing PartialEq; mission_dispatch.rs:542 assert_eq! fails to compile","description":"mission_dispatch.rs:542 uses assert_eq! on crate::events::Event values, but the Event enum in events.rs:146 doesn't derive PartialEq. Rustc suggests #[derive(PartialEq)]. Blocks cargo check/build of frankenterm-core lib. Fix: either derive PartialEq on Event (if all variants are Eq-able) or rewrite the assertion to compare specific fields via pattern match.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:01:54.113665Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T18:12:04.478409Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T18:12:04.478033Z","close_reason":"Fixed: crates/frankenterm-core/src/mission_dispatch.rs:542-560 was rewritten to match+destructure Event::WorkflowCompleted instead of assert_eq, with an inline comment citing ft-zv3u9 and the rationale that Event payload includes Detection/UserVarPayload which would cascade PartialEq across a wide surface. Event still doesn't derive PartialEq, and the test now compiles without needing it. Verified by cargo check --lib --tests passing past the prior E0369.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
